questions.scenario:
A developer pushes a commit to a branch named `feature/login`. The repository has this workflow:

```yaml
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
```

The developer checks the Actions tab and sees no workflow run. They open a PR from `feature/login` into `main`, and the workflow starts. What is the best explanation?

options.text: The push event only fires on the branch named in the `branches` filter, which is `main`. The push to `feature/login` did not match. The PR triggers because its base branch is `main`.
GitHub Actions does not run on feature branches. Workflows only work on the default branch.
The workflow file must exist on `feature/login` for push events to fire, and it was not committed there yet.
Push events are disabled by default. Only pull_request events work without additional configuration.

questions.explanation: The `branches` filter under `push` restricts the event to pushes that land directly on `main`. A push to `feature/login` does not match. The `pull_request` filter checks the base (target) branch of the PR, which is `main`, so it fires. Option C is plausible but incorrect here because the filter, not the file location, is the gating factor.

questions.scenario:
A workflow has three jobs:

```yaml
jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps: [...]
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: lint
    steps: [...]
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: test
    steps: [...]
```

The `lint` job fails because of a formatting error. What happens to `test` and `deploy`?

options.text: Both `test` and `deploy` are cancelled immediately and show a 'Cancelled' status.
Both `test` and `deploy` are skipped. They never start because their upstream dependency failed.
`test` is skipped, but `deploy` still runs because it only depends on `test`, not `lint`.
`test` runs anyway because `needs` is only advisory, and `deploy` is skipped.

questions.explanation: When a job fails, all downstream jobs in the dependency chain are skipped by default. `test` depends on `lint`, so it is skipped. `deploy` depends on `test`, and since `test` was skipped (not successful), `deploy` is also skipped. The failure cascades through the entire chain. Option C is wrong because `deploy` transitively depends on `lint` through `test`.

questions.scenario:
An engineer pushes three commits in quick succession to the `main` branch. The repository has this configuration:

```yaml
concurrency:
  group: ci-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true
```

They check the Actions tab and see that the first two runs show 'Cancelled' and only the third run is active. What caused this?

options.text: GitHub Actions has a limit of one concurrent run per repository, so older runs are always cancelled.
The `concurrency` group groups all three runs under the same key (`ci-refs/heads/main`). With `cancel-in-progress: true`, each new run cancels the previous one.
The pushes happened too fast and GitHub rate-limited the first two.
The first two workflows had syntax errors and were automatically rejected.

questions.explanation: The `concurrency` key groups runs by a key expression. Since all three pushes target `main`, the group evaluates to the same string. With `cancel-in-progress: true`, GitHub cancels the older in-progress or pending run when a new one enters the same group. This saves runner minutes on builds that are already outdated. Option A is wrong because the limit is per-group, not per-repository.

questions.scenario: A workflow runs on `push` and creates a new Git tag using `GITHUB_TOKEN`. The team expects a second workflow (triggered by `on: create`) to fire when the tag is created, but nothing happens. What is the most likely explanation?

options.text: The `on: create` event does not support tags, only branches.
Events generated by the default `GITHUB_TOKEN` do not trigger other workflows. This prevents infinite recursive loops.
Tags must be created from the GitHub web UI, not from workflows.
The second workflow file has a syntax error that prevents it from running.

questions.explanation: This is an intentional safety measure. If actions taken with the default GITHUB_TOKEN could trigger other workflows, a push-triggered workflow that creates a commit would trigger itself again, creating an infinite loop. To chain workflows via tags or commits, use a Personal Access Token (PAT) or a GitHub App token instead. Option A is wrong because `on: create` does support both tags and branches.

questions.scenario:
A workflow uses a matrix strategy to test across multiple Node.js versions:

```yaml
strategy:
  matrix:
    node-version: [18, 20, 22]
```

The build for Node 18 fails because a dependency dropped support for it. What happens to the Node 20 and Node 22 builds by default?

options.text: All three jobs run independently. The Node 20 and 22 builds continue and may succeed.
By default, GitHub cancels the remaining matrix jobs when any single variant fails (`fail-fast: true`).
Matrix jobs always run to completion regardless of individual failures.
The entire workflow is retried from the beginning with all three versions.

questions.explanation: The default behavior for matrix strategies is `fail-fast: true`, which cancels all other in-progress matrix jobs as soon as one fails. If you want all variants to complete (for example, to see the full compatibility picture), you must explicitly set `fail-fast: false`. Option A describes the behavior with `fail-fast: false`, not the default.

questions.scenario: Your team wants to run a workflow both on a schedule (every night at 2 AM UTC) and when a developer manually clicks 'Run workflow' in the GitHub UI. Which event triggers should be present in the `on` block?

options.text: `schedule` with a cron expression
`workflow_dispatch`
`repository_dispatch`
`push` with a time-based filter

questions.explanation: `schedule` uses POSIX cron syntax to trigger workflows at specific times. `workflow_dispatch` adds a 'Run workflow' button to the Actions tab for manual execution. `repository_dispatch` is for external API calls, not manual UI clicks. There is no time-based filter on `push` events.

questions.scenario: A job uses `permissions: { contents: read }` explicitly. The job also tries to push a commit using `GITHUB_TOKEN`. The push command fails with a 403 error. What is the root cause?

options.text: When you declare an explicit `permissions` block, it overrides all defaults. Since `contents` was set to `read`, the token cannot write (push) to the repository.
The GITHUB_TOKEN can never push commits. It is read-only by design.
The 403 error means the repository is archived and does not accept pushes.
Pushing commits requires the `packages: write` permission, not `contents: write`.

questions.explanation: An explicit `permissions` block replaces all inherited defaults. If you only grant `contents: read`, the token loses write access to the repository, even if the default would have included it. To push commits, you need `contents: write`. Option B is wrong because the GITHUB_TOKEN can push commits when it has the correct permissions.
